fixture.description: invalidate_all() was simplified to call _SESSIONS.clear() WITHOUT acquiring _LOCK, while get_session and put_session both read/mutate the module-level _SESSIONS dict under that same lock. Removing the guard means the clear is no longer serialized against concurrent get_session/put_session: the lock's mutual-exclusion contract is broken, so a clear can clobber state a concurrent writer just stored (lost/inconsistent state) and any compound clear-then-rebuild sequence is no longer atomic relative to other accessors. (Under CPython's GIL a single dict op is atomic so this rarely throws, but the synchronization invariant is still violated, and on free-threaded/no-GIL builds — or if a reader ever iterates the dict — it is an outright data race that can crash with 'dictionary changed size during iteration'.) Modified-file diff: the pre-bug `before` held _LOCK around the clear; the `after` removes that guard, introducing the unsynchronized mutation on the changed lines.

src/auth/session_store.py.before:
import threading
import time

_LOCK = threading.Lock()
_SESSIONS = {}
_TTL_SECONDS = 1800


def get_session(token):
    with _LOCK:
        entry = _SESSIONS.get(token)
        if entry is None:
            return None
        value, expires_at = entry
        if time.monotonic() > expires_at:
            del _SESSIONS[token]
            return None
        return value


def put_session(token, value):
    with _LOCK:
        _SESSIONS[token] = (value, time.monotonic() + _TTL_SECONDS)


def invalidate_all():
    with _LOCK:
        _SESSIONS.clear()

grader.rubric: PASS only if the MAIN report (not an appendix) flags that invalidate_all() in src/auth/session_store.py mutates the shared module-level _SESSIONS dict via _SESSIONS.clear() WITHOUT holding _LOCK, while get_session and put_session read/mutate that same dict under _LOCK -> the clear is no longer synchronized/serialized against concurrent readers and writers (broken mutual-exclusion contract; e.g. clobbering state a writer just stored, lost/inconsistent state, non-atomic relative to other accessors — and a data race / possible 'dictionary changed size during iteration' on free-threaded builds or if the dict is iterated), and names the fix as acquiring the SAME _LOCK around the clear (wrap it in `with _LOCK:`). Severity must be IMPORTANT or CRITICAL. A report that correctly notes individual dict operations are atomic under the GIL but still flags the broken locking invariant (unsynchronized shared-state mutation / unsafe under free-threading) PASSES — do NOT require the report to assert a crash or a mid-iteration failure as the mechanism. FAIL if the unlocked mutation is absent, graded below IMPORTANT, surfaced only as a minor/appendix note, or dismissed as pre-existing. Do NOT require the exact words 'data race'.

expected_behavior: Bugs/Security agent flags invalidate_all() calling _SESSIONS.clear() outside _LOCK as an unsynchronized mutation of shared state that the lock-guarded get_session/put_session protect, breaking the locking invariant
Names the fix as taking the same _LOCK (wrap the clear in `with _LOCK:`)
Reports it at IMPORTANT or CRITICAL severity in the main report
expected_not_behavior: Does NOT claim get_session/put_session are themselves unsynchronized (they correctly hold _LOCK)
Does NOT demand a different locking primitive (RLock/ConcurrentDict) when reusing the existing _LOCK is the minimal fix
Does NOT drop the finding as pre-existing
Does NOT require asserting a runtime crash or mid-iteration failure as the mechanism (a GIL-aware report that flags the broken locking invariant without claiming a crash is correct)
